article_title: Hiroshima University Researchers Crack the Code for 3D Printing Tungsten Carbide, One of Industry's Hardest Materials

content_preview.title: Hiroshima University Researchers Crack the Code for 3D Printing Tungsten Carbide, One of Industry's Hardest Materials
content_preview.summary: A Hiroshima University team used a hot-wire laser technique to 3D print defect-free tungsten carbide-cobalt exceeding 1,400 HV hardness, opening a path to cheaper, less wasteful production of cutting tools and industrial components.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Researchers at Hiroshima University have demonstrated a new additive manufacturing method capable of producing defect-free tungsten carbide-cobalt (WC-Co), one of the hardest engineered materials in industrial use. The technique, published in the International Journal of Refractory Metals and Hard Materials (April 2026 print edition), achieved hardness exceeding 1,400 HV on the Vickers scale -- a level surpassed only by materials such as sapphire and diamond.

The breakthrough addre...

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent materials science article with clear structure, good technical depth, and honest acknowledgment of limitations. Well-organized into what we know, why it matters, and what remains uncertain.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 sources fetched and verified. ScienceDaily confirms Marumoto leadership, Hiroshima University, hot-wire laser technique, WC-Co material, 1400+ HV hardness, IJRMHM April 2026 publication, Mitsubishi collaboration. Interesting Engineering confirms rod-leading vs laser-leading approaches, Marumoto quote on material costs, nickel alloy interlayer solution. Tom's Hardware confirms defect-free result and softening-not-melting approach.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified against sources. Technical details (1400 HV, rod-leading vs laser-leading, nickel interlayer, temperature control) all confirmed. Marumoto quote matches verbatim.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. The 'sensationalist language' warning is a false positive — the word 'incredible' appears in the Tom's Hardware URL slug, not in the article body text. Article tone is measured and appropriate.
editor_notes.originality: No prior coverage of tungsten carbide 3D printing or this research group in the archive.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality materials science article with solid sourcing and balanced reporting. Approved.
